skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation skill for AI agents using inference.sh. It opens pages, interacts with element refs, captures screenshots or video, uploads files, uses proxies, and runs page JavaScript.
skill.summary: Automates browser sessions with refs, screenshots, video, proxies, file upload, and page JavaScript execution.
skill.author: toolshell

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, credential, proxy, and screenshot patterns. Most command hits are documentation examples for the intended inference.sh browser workflow, but the skill genuinely supports sensitive browser automation, including authenticated sessions, cookie extraction, local file upload, proxy routing, JavaScript execution, screenshots, and video. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious intent was found, so this is high risk rather than a critical block.

high_findings.title: Authenticated Session and Cookie Exposure Risk
high_findings.description: The skill documents filling login credentials, OAuth passwords, 2FA codes, reusable authenticated sessions, and cookie extraction. This is legitimate browser automation, but it can expose credentials, session cookies, and protected data if used on private accounts or untrusted pages.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly show credential entry and cookie extraction. The file also contains safety guidance, so the risk is capability-based rather than confirmed malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Browser JavaScript Execution
high_findings.description: The execute function accepts JavaScript code and runs it in the page context. This can extract page data, click hidden elements, or access cookies available to page JavaScript when used on authenticated or sensitive sites.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The functionality is explicitly documented and semantically powerful. Legitimate extraction examples exist, but the same interface can be misused on sensitive pages.

high_findings.title: Proxy Rotation and Anonymized Scraping Support
high_findings.description: The proxy documentation includes proxy rotation for web scraping and SOCKS proxy routing for privacy. These are legitimate in testing contexts, but they can enable evasion of rate limits, attribution controls, or site access policies.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text directly describes rotating proxies for scraping and privacy proxy routing. The document also advises delays, so this is high risk but not proof of malicious intent.

high_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
high_findings.description: The interact function supports uploading local file paths to web pages. This is expected for browser testing, but it can disclose local files if an agent is instructed to upload sensitive paths.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file upload action and file_paths field are explicitly documented. The evidence does not show malicious target selection, so the confidence reflects capability risk.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots and Video May Capture Sensitive Data
medium_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and full browser session videos. The documentation warns not to record sensitive sessions, but users could still capture credentials, personal data, admin pages, or protected records.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The recording behavior and sensitive-data warning are explicit. This is a moderate concern because the docs include mitigation guidance and recording is optional.

medium_findings.title: Shell Templates Execute Browser Commands and Write Outputs
medium_findings.description: The shell templates run infsh, parse results with jq, create output directories, and save screenshots, text, links, and optional video metadata. This is intended behavior, but it increases filesystem and command-execution exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited script performs command execution and local writes. It does not show obfuscation or hidden exfiltration, so the concern is operational exposure.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Intended Examples
low_findings.description: The many backtick, command substitution, and shell invocation hits are primarily Markdown and shell examples for the intended inference.sh workflow. They are not hidden payloads, but users should still review commands before running copied templates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms the patterns are visible examples and shell templates. The confidence is high that most static command hits are false positives for malicious execution.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Searched the analyzed skill files for override, system-update, skip-review, and similar instruction phrases. No evidence found of prompt injection attempts in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The review used targeted search across SKILL.md, references, and templates. Absence of evidence is not absolute proof, but no suspicious instruction text was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Access to infsh Browser Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash access to infsh commands, enabling remote browser sessions from agent instructions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and quick-start command directly establish the executable browser-control pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Handling in Browser Forms
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation provides patterns for filling passwords and 2FA codes in browser sessions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show password and TOTP handling. They are visible examples, but the capability is sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Screenshots, Video, and Extracted Page Data Saved Locally
dangerous_patterns.description: The capture workflow saves screenshots, page text, links, elements, and optional video data to local output files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The template clearly writes captured browser outputs to files. This is expected behavior but can preserve sensitive data.

content.user_title: Automate Browser Workflows with Agent Browser
content.value_statement: AI agents often need a real browser to inspect pages, submit forms, and capture evidence. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable inference.sh workflow for browser automation.
actual_capabilities: Opens web pages and returns compact element refs for links, buttons, inputs, and other interactive controls.
Clicks, fills, types, selects, scrolls, drags, uploads files, and navigates by using returned element refs.
Captures viewport or full-page screenshots and can record browser sessions as video.
Runs JavaScript in the page context to extract text, links, tables, counts, or computed page values.
Supports authenticated browsing patterns, 2FA handling, session reuse, and proxy configuration.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and access to the hosted agent-browser app.
Element refs become stale after navigation or dynamic page changes and require a fresh snapshot.
Automating authenticated sites can expose credentials, cookies, screenshots, or video if used carelessly.
Proxy rotation and scraping workflows must respect site terms, rate limits, and local law.

use_cases.title: Validate Web Forms
use_cases.description: Open a form, inspect element refs, submit realistic inputs, and capture screenshots of success or failure states.

use_cases.title: Collect Page Evidence
use_cases.description: Capture page text, links, screenshots, and optional video from public pages for later review.

use_cases.title: Automate Authenticated Checks
use_cases.description: Log in with environment-provided credentials, verify dashboard state, and preserve session evidence when needed.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Inspect a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to open the target URL, list the interactive element refs, summarize the page title, and close the session.

prompt_templates.title: Capture a Page Snapshot
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the URL with agent-browser, take a full-page screenshot, extract visible page text, save the key findings, and close the session.

prompt_templates.title: Submit a Test Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this form, identify the refs for each field, fill only the provided test data, submit it, verify the result, and capture a screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Authenticated Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to log in with approved environment variables, avoid recording credentials, verify the protected page, extract non-sensitive status data, and close the session.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and capture page evidence.
output_examples.output: The workflow returns the page title, current URL, visible interactive refs, extracted text highlights, and screenshot file references.

output_examples.input: Verify a login form with test credentials.
output_examples.output: The workflow reports which refs were used, whether navigation left the login page, and where the verification screenshot was saved.

output_examples.input: Record a short settings workflow for documentation.
output_examples.output: The workflow returns a browser session video reference after close, plus notes about the actions performed.

best_practices: Use test accounts and non-sensitive data whenever possible.
Re-snapshot after navigation, form submission, or dynamic content changes.
Disable video recording when credentials, tokens, personal data, or private records may appear.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode passwords, proxy credentials, or session cookies in prompts or scripts.
Do not use proxy rotation to bypass rate limits, access controls, or site terms.
Do not upload local files unless the target site and file contents are explicitly approved.

faq.question: What does this skill automate?
faq.answer: It automates browser navigation, element interaction, screenshots, video recording, file upload, proxies, and page JavaScript through inference.sh.

faq.question: Does it require extra software?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires the inference.sh CLI and access to the agent-browser app.

faq.question: Can it handle login workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes username, password, OAuth, 2FA, and session reuse patterns.

faq.question: Can it execute JavaScript?
faq.answer: Yes. It can run JavaScript in the page context, which is powerful and must be limited to approved sites.

faq.question: Why is this skill high risk?
faq.answer: It can browse arbitrary URLs, handle credentials, upload local files, route traffic through proxies, and capture sensitive page content.

faq.question: Is there evidence of prompt injection in the skill files?
faq.answer: No evidence found during review of the analyzed files.
